channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: One of Neptune's 16 moons is not like the others, James Webb telescope finds — and it could be key to fully understanding the solar system - Live Science
item.description:
1. One of Neptune's 16 moons is not like the others, James Webb telescope finds — and it could be key to fully understanding the solar system  Live Science
  2. Astronomers believe Neptunian moon is lone intact survivor of ancient collision  CNN
  3. Neptune’s Moon Nereid Might Be the Sole Intact Survivor of an Ancient Lunar Collision  Smithsonian Magazine
  4. This Moon of Neptune Might Have Survived a Wrecking-Ball Event  The New York Times
  5. Neptune's odd moon Nereid may be sole survivor from ancient cosmic shake-up  Yahoo

  3. AI Is Becoming A Scientist: How Self-Driving Labs Will Accelerate Discovery  Bernard Marr
  4. With AI, science is borderless  BusinessLine
item.source: American Academy of Arts and Sciences

item.title: A joint European-Chinese satellite just went up on Vega-C, and the images it returns could change how scientists understand Earth’s magnetic shield - Space Daily

item.title: Scientists just reversed about 80% of aging in elderly mice in a single month — and they did it by boosting one protein - Space Daily

  4. Scientists say restoring a fading protein helped older mice push back against frailty  The Cool Down

item.title: Scientists thought Jupiter's moon Europa was ejecting water. Now they're not so sure - Space

item.title: Complex animal life may be 10 million years older than scientists thought — and a new fossil site in northwestern Canada suggests it all started in the deep sea, not the shallow waters where most early fossils show up - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Complex animal life may be 10 million years older than scientists thought — and a new fossil site in northwestern Canada suggests it all started in the deep sea, not the shallow waters where most early fossils show up  Space Daily
  2. Discovery of new fossils in Northwest Canada changes view of early animal evolution  Phys.org
  3. Complex animals evolved up to 10 million years earlier than previously thought, fossil discovery shows  Live Science
  4. The Oldest Evidence of Animal Sex Has Been Found, and It’s Mind-Boggling  404 Media
  5. These bizarre fossils represent some of the earliest moving, sexually reproducing life ever discovered  Scientific American

item.title: Scientists May Finally Know Why T. Rex Had Such Tiny Arms - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Scientists May Finally Know Why T. Rex Had Such Tiny Arms  ScienceAlert
  2. Why meat-eating dinosaurs like T. rex evolved tiny arms  Phys.org
  3. Tyrannosaurus Rex’s tiny arms finally explained by scientists  News.com.au
  4. Why were T. rex’s arms so tiny? Paleontologists finally find an answer.  Popular Science
  5. T. rex didn't evolve tiny arms because its body got bigger — it evolved tiny arms because its jaws got more powerful, according to a new study of 82 meat-eating dinosaur species that found the same pattern repeating across giant predators  Space Daily

item.title: In 1970 a Soviet probe became the first object ever to transmit from the surface of another planet, and it lasted barely 23 minutes on Venus before the heat killed it - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 1970 a Soviet probe became the first object ever to transmit from the surface of another planet, and it lasted barely 23 minutes on Venus before the heat killed it  Space Daily
  2. In 1982 the Soviet Union landed a probe on the surface of Venus that survived 127 minutes in heat that melts lead and pressure dense enough to crush a submarine — long enough to scan back two panoramas of flat basaltic rock under an orange-tinted sky b  Space Daily
  3. 57 years and one day ago, the Soviet probe Venera 6 traversed the clouds of Venus for 51 minutes under parachutes and stopped transmitting 10 kilometers from the surface because the pressure of 60 bar and the heat of 320 degrees Celsius crushed its 405-  CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
  4. The Soviet Venera probes are still sitting on the surface of Venus, slowly being crushed and corroded by 900-degree sulfuric acid clouds, and the photographs they sent back in 1975 remain the only images humans have ever taken from the surface of another  Space Daily

item.title: The Parker Solar Probe is still flying through the Sun's corona at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in about 20 seconds, because a 4.5-inch carbon-foam shield keeps the spacecraft in the shade - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Parker Solar Probe is still flying through the Sun's corona at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in about 20 seconds, because a 4.5-inch carbon-foam shield keeps the spacecraft in the shade  Space Daily
  2. NASA sent a spacecraft toward the sun - then the impossible happened  MSN
  3. The Parker Solar Probe is now moving at 430,000 miles per hour, fast enough to cross the continental United States in 20 seconds, and it survives skimming the Sun's corona because a four-and-a-half-inch slab of carbon foam stays at room temperature on the  Space Daily
